meta.title: SUDEP Personalised Risk (Jha)
meta.outcome: Relative risk of sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP) for an individual, combining 22 routine clinical factors, expressed as a multiple of the risk of an average person with epilepsy (Bayesian logistic model of Jha et al., reproduced from the published adjusted odds ratios).
meta.cff_info:
What this shows. This tool reproduces the 22-factor Bayesian logistic model of Jha et al. (Neurology 2021), developed on 1,273 people (287 SUDEP cases, 986 controls) from four cohorts. It combines many routine clinical factors — not just convulsion frequency — into a single estimate of an individual's relative risk of SUDEP.

How to read it

  • The result is a relative risk multiplier (e.g. ×3) compared with a baseline/average person with epilepsy — not an absolute probability of death.
  • The chart shows each selected factor's multiplicative effect (right/blue = raises risk, left/green = lowers it), mirroring the paper's adjusted odds-ratio figure; the factors combine to the overall relative risk.
  • The strongest modifiable factors are generalized tonic-clonic and focal seizure frequency and poor medication adherence. Younger age at onset, alcohol excess, male sex and a family history also raise the estimate.

Important limitations

  • Relative, not absolute. The published data give the adjusted odds ratios but not the intercept/population-risk offset, so an absolute SUDEP probability (per person-year) cannot be reproduced here. As a rough anchor, average SUDEP incidence is on the order of ~1 per 1,000 people with epilepsy per year.
  • The model has moderate discrimination (AUROC 0.72) and the authors emphasise that risk is a probability distribution with wide uncertainty, and recommend the tool be used by clinical researchers pending further external validation.
  • Medication associations are adjusted and almost certainly non-causal (markers of severe/refractory epilepsy) — never change treatment based on this tool.

Demonstration and research-communication aid only — not a substitute for specialist assessment or an individual prediction of death.

predictors.name: Epilepsy aetiology
predictors.assess: Underlying cause of the epilepsy (harmonised across the source studies; reference = symptomatic). Adjusted log-ORs: idiopathic −0.15, cryptogenic −0.44, undetermined/unknown +0.58. Aetiology estimates are uncertain and anticorrelated (poor identifiability).

predictors.name: Age at epilepsy onset
predictors.assess: Age (years) at epilepsy onset. Younger onset carries higher SUDEP risk. Modelled on its natural scale from the paper's Figure 3 (adjusted per-SD log-OR −0.34; ≈ −0.025 per year), referenced to the cohort-average onset age (~18 years, relative risk 1 there).

predictors.name: Current age
predictors.assess: Current age / age at endpoint (years). Older current age carries slightly higher risk once onset age is held constant. Natural-scale slope from Figure 3 (adjusted per-SD log-OR +0.15; ≈ +0.012 per year), referenced to the cohort-average age (~35 years).

predictors.name: Sex
predictors.assess: Biological sex. Male sex is associated with slightly higher SUDEP risk (adjusted log-OR +0.24; reference = female).

predictors.name: Family history of epilepsy
predictors.assess: First-degree relative with epilepsy. Associated with higher SUDEP risk (adjusted log-OR +0.66); present in ~4% of the cohort.
predictors.group_info: Tap any that apply. Each is an independent adjusted factor from the model; family history and learning disability raise SUDEP risk, while a documented cardiac or respiratory comorbidity was associated with slightly lower recorded SUDEP risk in this cohort.

predictors.name: Respiratory comorbidity
predictors.assess: Documented respiratory comorbidity (e.g. asthma, COPD). Associated with LOWER modelled risk (adjusted log-OR −0.57) — likely a competing-risk or confounding artefact rather than protection; interpret with caution.

predictors.name: Cardiac comorbidity
predictors.assess: Documented cardiac comorbidity (heart disease). Associated with LOWER modelled risk (adjusted log-OR −0.56) — probably confounded (e.g. deaths reclassified as cardiac, or MRI misclassification); not evidence of protection.

predictors.name: Learning disability
predictors.assess: Learning disability / intellectual disability (IQ<70 or too impaired to test). Small positive association after adjustment (adjusted log-OR +0.11) — much weaker than in unadjusted analyses once seizure frequency is accounted for.

predictors.name: Previous epilepsy surgery
predictors.assess: History of neurosurgery to treat the epilepsy. Associated with lower modelled risk (adjusted log-OR −0.49), consistent with a protective role of successful surgery.

predictors.name: Alcohol or drug excess
predictors.assess: Documented alcohol excess/abuse or drug abuse. A modifiable factor associated with higher risk (adjusted log-OR +0.59).

predictors.name: GTCS frequency
predictors.assess: Frequency of generalized tonic-clonic seizures (incl. focal-to-bilateral tonic-clonic), seizures per month. The single strongest modifiable driver (adjusted log-OR +0.61 per 10/month). At 30 GTCS/month the relative risk is ≈6× (matches the paper's Figure 3).

predictors.name: Focal seizure frequency
predictors.assess: Frequency of focal-onset seizures without progression to bilateral tonic-clonic, seizures per month. Independently associated with higher risk (adjusted log-OR +0.62 per 10/month) — a novel finding of this model, that non-convulsive seizure burden also matters.

predictors.name: ASM adherence
predictors.assess: Adherence to antiseizure medication. GOOD adherence is associated with substantially lower SUDEP risk (adjusted log-OR −1.03) — one of the two strongest modifiable factors alongside seizure frequency. Reference = poor/unknown adherence.

predictors.name: Phenytoin
predictors.assess: Currently prescribed phenytoin at the endpoint (mono- or polytherapy). Adjusted log-OR +0.33 in the model. NB associations between individual ASMs and SUDEP are adjusted for seizure frequency and adherence and are almost certainly non-causal — they likely mark treatment-resistant epilepsy or drug side-effects, not a harmful drug effect. Do NOT stop or change medication on the basis of this tool.
predictors.group_info: Tap every antiseizure medication the patient currently takes. Each carries its own adjusted odds ratio from the model (some markers of more severe epilepsy, e.g. being on several drugs, associate with higher SUDEP risk). These are associations, not causal effects — do not stop a medication on this basis.

predictors.name: Carbamazepine
predictors.assess: Currently prescribed carbamazepine at the endpoint (mono- or polytherapy). Adjusted log-OR +0.66 in the model. NB associations between individual ASMs and SUDEP are adjusted for seizure frequency and adherence and are almost certainly non-causal — they likely mark treatment-resistant epilepsy or drug side-effects, not a harmful drug effect. Do NOT stop or change medication on the basis of this tool.

NB associations between individual ASMs and SUDEP are adjusted for seizure frequency and adherence and are almost certainly non-causal — they likely mark treatment-resistant epilepsy or drug side-effects, not a harmful drug effect. Do NOT stop or change medication on the basis of this tool.

Do NOT stop or change medication on the basis of this tool.

model.panel_title: How each factor changes SUDEP risk
model.rr_ref_label: vs an average person with epilepsy
model.note: Relative risk only (a multiple of average SUDEP risk), NOT an absolute probability of death. Average SUDEP incidence is roughly 1 per 1,000 people with epilepsy per year. Research/education aid — the authors advise use by clinical researchers pending further validation.
model.viz_info: Each factor you select contributes a multiplicative effect to SUDEP risk (the adjusted odds ratio from the model): bars to the right (blue) raise risk, bars to the left (green) lower it. Multiplying them together gives the combined relative risk versus a baseline person with epilepsy. Continuous factors (ages, seizure frequencies) are shown on their natural scale from the paper's Figure 3.
model.baseline_note: Illustrative only. Jha's model gives a RELATIVE risk; this absolute figure assumes an average SUDEP incidence of ~1.2 per 1,000 people with epilepsy per year, which itself varies widely (~0.9–2.3 in community cohorts, higher in drug-resistant epilepsy). It is not an individual prediction of death.

provenance.source: Jha et al., Neurology 2021;96(21):e2627-e2638 (doi 10.1212/WNL.0000000000011849) — 22 posterior adjusted log-odds-ratios from Dryad Additional Material Table e5 (Full model, priors N(0,1)); continuous-factor natural scales from paper Figure 3; means from supplement Table e1.
provenance.notes: Relative-risk reproduction. RR = exp(Σ βj·xj) vs a reference profile (symptomatic aetiology, cohort-mean ages ~18/35, zero seizures, female, no comorbidity/surgery/alcohol/family-history, poor adherence, no listed ASM), which gives RR=1. Coefficients (Table e5, σ=1): aetiology idiopathic −0.15/symptomatic 0/cryptogenic −0.44/unknown +0.58; age-onset −0.34/SD; age-endpoint +0.15/SD; male +0.24; family-hx +0.66; respiratory −0.57; cardiac −0.56; learning-dis +0.11; surgery −0.49; alcohol +0.59; GTCS +0.61 per (per-month/10); focal +0.62 per (per-month/10); good-adherence −1.03; phenytoin +0.33; carbamazepine +0.66; valproate +0.19; benzodiazepines +0.54; barbiturates +0.12; lamotrigine +0.66; vigabatrin +0.84; topiramate +0.41; gabapentin +0.38. Continuous predictors modelled on natural scale: frequencies as coef/10 per month (validated vs Fig 3: 30/mo → RR ≈6.2); ages via 2-point splines anchored at cohort-mean (onset slope −0.0254/yr, current-age slope +0.0123/yr, derived from Fig 3 crossovers/endpoints with means from Table e1). ABSOLUTE risk NOT reproducible: Dryad supplement gives coefficients only, not the center intercepts or the population-risk offset (background 1.2/1000 py) needed for Fig-4 absolute risk. AUROC 0.72. Authors restrict to clinical researchers pending external validation. Extracted 2026-07-08 from user-provided Dryad Additional Material PDF.
